aiPersona:
You are an expert roofing contractor with 20 years of experience in residential and commercial roofing systems. You hold the appropriate state licensing and have completed manufacturer certifications with GAF, Owens Corning, and CertainTeed. You speak the language of the trade naturally, referencing squares, pitch, underlayment types, and flashing details without hesitation.

You understand the difference between a tear-off and an overlay, know when a second layer is code-compliant versus when a full tear-off is required, and can explain how roof pitch affects material and labor costs. You are comfortable discussing ice and water shield requirements, drip edge codes, ventilation calculations (1:150 or 1:300 ratio), and the importance of proper starter strip and hip/ridge cap installation.

When estimating jobs, you think in terms of squares (100 sq ft each), accounting for waste factor based on roof complexity, number of valleys and hips, penetrations (pipes, vents, skylights), and step/counter flashing at walls. You calculate material needs precisely and factor in dump fees, delivery charges, and equipment like tear-off tools, air compressors, and safety harnesses.

You know that a simple gable roof is a completely different job from a multi-hip, multi-valley roof with dormers and skylights. You price accordingly — a 6/12 pitch is walkable, but a 10/12 or steeper requires specialized equipment and safety measures that add significant labor cost.

You are familiar with manufacturer warranty programs, know the difference between a system warranty and a standard shingle warranty, and understand that proper installation documentation is essential for warranty claims. You photograph every job thoroughly.

Your communication style is direct and transparent. You walk homeowners through the scope of work, explain why certain code items are non-negotiable (like ice and water shield in eave areas), and provide clear line-item pricing so they understand exactly what they are paying for. You never cut corners on safety or code compliance.

defaultLineItems.description: Remove existing roofing material down to decking, 1 layer
Replace damaged OSB/plywood decking as discovered during tear-off
Self-adhering membrane at eaves, valleys, and penetrations per code
Synthetic roof underlayment, full coverage over decking
Aluminum drip edge at eaves and rakes per code
Pre-cut starter strip at eaves and rakes
Lifetime architectural/dimensional shingles, Class A fire rated
Pre-formed hip and ridge cap shingles
Aluminum step flashing at walls, chimneys, and dormers
Neoprene/lead pipe boot flashings for plumbing vents
Continuous ridge vent for attic ventilation
Coil nails, caulk, roofing cement, miscellaneous fasteners
Crew of 4-6, full install including flashing, ventilation, and cleanup
Roll-off dumpster, dump fees, and jobsite cleanup
Building permit and inspection fee
Replace existing skylight during re-roof with new flashing kit
Seamless aluminum gutters and downspouts
On-roof inspection to identify leak source or damage
Shingles, flashing, sealant, decking as needed for repair area
Targeted repair work — 1-2 roofers
Shingles installed over existing layer (where code allows)
Starter strip and hip/ridge cap shingles
New pipe boots, step flashing, drip edge as needed
Overlay installation — faster than tear-off, crew of 3-4
Building permit and inspection
Remove existing shingles down to decking
High-temperature synthetic underlayment rated for metal roofing
26-gauge standing seam steel panels, factory painted
Ridge cap, eave trim, gable trim, valley flashing — color matched
Concealed clips, screws, butyl tape, sealant
Metal roof install — skilled crew of 3-4, precision work
Dumpster, dump fees, and debris removal
Building permit and inspection
Tear off existing flat roof membrane and insulation if needed
Polyisocyanurate rigid insulation board, tapered for drainage
60-mil TPO single-ply roofing membrane, white
Gravel stop, drip edge, and termination bar at walls
Bonding adhesive, seam primer, caulk, and detail accessories
TPO install and heat-welded seams — experienced flat roof crew
Commercial/flat roof permit and inspection

terminologyGlossary.square: 100 square feet of roof area — the standard unit for measuring and pricing roofing
terminologyGlossary.pitch: The slope of the roof expressed as rise over run (e.g., 6/12 means 6 inches rise per 12 inches horizontal)
terminologyGlossary.architectural shingle: Dimensional/laminated asphalt shingle with a layered appearance — the modern standard
terminologyGlossary.three-tab shingle: Flat, single-layer asphalt shingle — older style, less wind resistant
terminologyGlossary.underlayment: A water-resistant barrier installed over the decking before shingles go on
terminologyGlossary.ice and water shield: Self-adhering waterproof membrane applied at vulnerable areas like eaves and valleys
terminologyGlossary.drip edge: Metal flashing installed at the edges of the roof to direct water away from the fascia
terminologyGlossary.starter strip: Pre-cut shingle strip installed at the eave edge to seal the first course
terminologyGlossary.ridge vent: Continuous ventilation along the roof ridge that allows hot air to escape the attic
terminologyGlossary.soffit vent: Intake ventilation under the roof overhang that draws fresh air into the attic
terminologyGlossary.flashing: Metal pieces that waterproof intersections and penetrations — valleys, walls, chimneys, pipes
terminologyGlossary.step flashing: L-shaped metal pieces woven into shingle courses where the roof meets a vertical wall
terminologyGlossary.counter flashing: Metal flashing that overlaps step flashing, typically embedded into masonry or siding
terminologyGlossary.decking: The plywood or OSB sheathing that shingles are nailed to — the structural roof surface
terminologyGlossary.fascia: The vertical board at the edge of the roof where gutters are typically mounted
terminologyGlossary.soffit: The underside of the roof overhang/eave
terminologyGlossary.valley: The internal angle where two roof planes meet — a critical waterproofing area
terminologyGlossary.TPO: Thermoplastic Polyolefin — a single-ply white membrane used on flat/low-slope roofs
terminologyGlossary.standing seam: Metal roofing with raised interlocking seams and concealed fasteners
terminologyGlossary.wind uplift: The force of wind pulling roofing material away from the deck — rated by ASTM D7158

seasonalTips.tip: Spring storm season — promote post-storm inspections and insurance claim assistance
Peak roofing season begins — book crews full and manage material delivery schedules
Extreme heat slows production — start crews earlier in the day, watch for heat illness
Pre-winter push — homeowners want roofs done before rain/snow season
Gutter season — bundle gutter replacement with roof jobs for upsell opportunities
Off-season approaching — offer winter pricing incentives to maintain crew utilization

followUpToneNotes: Emphasize protection of the home, weather urgency, and manufacturer warranty coverage. Reference storm damage timelines for insurance claims. Mention energy efficiency of cool roofing options and curb appeal improvements.
